coredata.dc:title: Determination of the composition, encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity in protein drug delivery systems using circular dichroism spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Zhili
Li, Shanghao
Han, Xu
Al-Youbi, Abdulrahman O.
Bashammakh, Abdulaziz S.
El-Shahawi, Mohammad S.
Leblanc, Roger M.
coredata.dc:description: Peptides and proteins have become very promising drug candidates in recent decades due to their unique properties. However, the application of these drugs has been limited by their high enzymatic susceptibility, low membrane permeability and poor bioavailability when administered orally. Considerable efforts have been made to design and develop drug delivery systems that could transport peptides and proteins to targeted area. Although it is of great importance to determine the composition after loading a drug to the carrier, the ability to do so is significantly limited by current analytical methods. In this letter, five important proteins, α1-antitrypsin, hemoglobin human, human serum albumin, human transferrin and r-globulin were chemically conjugated to two model drug carriers, namely carbon dots and polymer O-(2-carboxyethyl) polyethylene glycol. A simple yet convenient method based on circular dichroism spectroscopy was developed to determine the compositions of the various protein-carrier conjugates.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Peptide and protein
Drug delivery
Encapsulation efficiency
Loading capacity
Circular dichroism spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
Syntheses and characterization of protein-C-Dots conjugates
Syntheses and characterization of protein-PEG conjugates
Selection of model drug carriers and protein drugs
CD spectra of free proteins and protein-drug carrier conjugates
Calibration curves for protein concentration determination
Encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity determination

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from King Abdulaziz University , Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, and University of Miami , USA. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Circular dichroism spectra of A1AT at concentrations from 0.0125 to 0.2 mg/ml in water (A), in the presence of 0.05 mg/ml of PEG (B) and in the presence of 0.05 mg/ml of C-Dots (C). See Supplementary Material for the spectra of Hb, rGb, HSA and HT.

Calibration curves of proteins using CD signal intensity versus concentration: (A), A1AT at 208 nm, (B), Hb at 208 nm and (C), rGb at 218 nm. See Supplementary Material for the calibration curves for HSA and HT.
CD spectra of PEG conjugated proteins, (A) and C-Dots conjugated proteins, (B).
Determination of compositions of A1AT-PEG and A1AT-C-Dots conjugates based on CD spectroscopy and lyophilization (please see Supplementary Material for the conjugates of the other four proteins).
Encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity of PEG and C-Dots with five different proteins based on CD spectroscopy and lyophilization.

head.ce:title: Determination of the composition, encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity in protein drug delivery systems using circular dichroism spectroscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Miami, 1301 Memorial Drive, Coral Gables, FL 33146, United States

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, P.O. Box 80203, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Peptides and proteins have become very promising drug candidates in recent decades due to their unique properties. However, the application of these drugs has been limited by their high enzymatic susceptibility, low membrane permeability and poor bioavailability when administered orally. Considerable efforts have been made to design and develop drug delivery systems that could transport peptides and proteins to targeted area. Although it is of great importance to determine the composition after loading a drug to the carrier, the ability to do so is significantly limited by current analytical methods. In this letter, five important proteins, α1-antitrypsin, hemoglobin human, human serum albumin, human transferrin and r-globulin were chemically conjugated to two model drug carriers, namely carbon dots and polymer O-(2-carboxyethyl) polyethylene glycol. A simple yet convenient method based on circular dichroism spectroscopy was developed to determine the compositions of the various protein-carrier conjugates.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the most promising fields in pharmaceutical industry is the selective preparation of macromolecules such as peptides and proteins to be used as drug candidates for disease treatments [1,2]. Compared to the traditional low-molecular-weight compounds (i.e., less than 500 g/mol), peptides and proteins are highly selective due to their rich structure features that allow the multiple points of contact with their target. The increased selectivity normally leads to better therapeutic activity and relatively fewer side effects. The market for peptide and protein drugs was estimated to be about 10% of the pharmaceutical market in 2013, and it continues growing much faster than the counterpart of small molecules [3]. Targeted drug delivery [4,5] by drug delivery system (DDS) has been the main strategy to overcome the problems (i.e., high enzymatic susceptibility and poor bioavailability) associated with peptide and protein drugs [6,7]. A properly designed DDS would provide alternative transportation mechanism and thus increase the ability to pass the bio-barriers. Thanks to the specific targeted transport, a DDS could improve the bio-distribution of peptide and protein drugs while minimizing their toxicity [8].

In targeted drug delivery, a drug is entrapped in or chemically linked to a DDS (i.e., nanoparticles or biodegradable polymers) that is designed to selectively deliver it to the site of interest. There are two main strategies to load peptide and protein drugs to a carrier: one is passive adsorption through non-covalent interactions and the other is via labile covalent bonding between the drug and the carrier [9]. In both strategies, one important aspect to consider when designing a DDS is the drug-to-carrier ratio since the use of high quantity of carrier could potentially cause a series of problems, such as carrier-related toxicity due to the poor metabolism and biodegradability of the carriers [10]. Also, the use of unreasonably high amount of carrier would increase the possibility of immune reactions against the carrier in the patient body [11]. Thus, the development of convenient and reliable analytical methods for the determination of drug-to-carrier ratio and the study of the detailed composition of a DDS is of great importance.

Traditionally, to determine the composition of a DDS, the protein-carrier conjugate is separated from the unbound protein by centrifugation after the drug loading process is completed. The amount of unbound protein (free drug) in the supernatant or bounded protein (loaded drug) after centrifugation is then determined by either UV–vis absorbance or dye-binding assays based on a known extinction coefficient or a standard calibration curve [12–14]. However, this method sometimes suffers from low reliability [15] presumably because of the possible interference from the drug carriers (i.e., nanoparticles). Most of nanoparticle drug carriers demonstrate strong UV–vis absorbance and often overlap with the ones of protein drugs, making the UV–vis quantification of protein drugs extremely difficult [16]. Also, nano drug carrier (<10 nm) usually has a strong fluorescence, complicating the fluorescence-based determination. Another shortcoming associated with this traditional method is that the determination of encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity of a DDS is based on bulk analysis [17,18], it does not exclude the contribution from the free carriers (i.e., carriers that do not carry any drugs).

Herein, we report a new approach to study the properties (i.e., composition, encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity) of chemically linked protein-carrier conjugates based on our previously developed circular dichroism (CD) spectroscopy determination method [19]. Since the approach is based on CD spectroscopy, it could well avoid the aforementioned problems associated with the traditional analytical techniques.

The chemicals of human serum albumin (HSA) and human transferrin (iron saturated, HT) were obtained from MP Biomedicals, LLC (Solon, OH). Carbon powder (99.99%) was purchased from Strem Chemicals Inc. (Newburyport, MA). ACS grade sulfuric acid (98%) and nitric acid (68–70%) were purchased from ARISTAR (distributed by VWR, Radnor, PA). O-(2-carboxyethyl) polyethylene glycol (PEG), r-Globulins from human blood (rGb), Hemoglobin human (Hb), α1-Antitrypsin from human plasma (A1AT), carbon nanopowder and other chemicals were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (St. Louis, MO). All of the chemicals above were used as received without any further purification. Dialysis tubing with a molecular weight cutoff of 3500 was purchased from Thermo Scientific (Rockford, IL). Size exclusion chromatography was performed using GE Healthcare Sephacryl S-300 (Uppsala, Sweden). Purified water was obtained from a Modulab 2020 water purification system (San Antonio, TX). The water has a surface tension of 72.6 mN m−1 and a resistivity of >18 MΩ cm at 20.0 ± 0.5 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (C-Dots) used in this study were synthesized and characterized according to a previously developed procedure [20]. The titration of C-Dots: 6.0 mg of C-Dots were dissolved in 60.0 ml of water and titrated by 0.00055 M NaOH solution (the NaOH solution used in titration was freshly prepared and standardized by KHP). When reaching the end point, 63.42 ml of this NaOH solution were used, which is about 0.03488 mmol of NaOH. Assuming only carboxylic group on C-Dots reacted with NaOH, it is estimated that there are 5.8 mmol of –COOH available for each gram of C-Dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses and characterization of protein-C-Dots conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: A typical procedure is as follows (A1AT-C-Dots conjugates): To a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (1.0 ml, pH = 7.4) of carbon dots (2.0 mg) at ambient temperature was added a PBS solution (1.0 mL) of A1AT (5.0 mg). The resulted solution was stirred for 10 min before a PBS solution (0.5 mL) of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 5.0 mg) was added dropwise. The mixture was then allowed to stir at the same temperature for 2 h before it was subjected to a size-exclusion column for separation and purification. The elution from the size-exclusion column was collected into small vials and then characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog–3) before used in the circular dichroism spectroscopy study (JASCO J–810 spectropolarimeter). After lyophilization, 6.05 mg of A1AT-C-Dots conjugate were obtained, resulting in an 86.4% yield. The conjugates of C-Dots with other four proteins were synthesized following the same procedures: for Hb-C-Dots conjugate, 15.0 mg of Hb were reacted with 6.0 mg of C-Dots; after purification and lyophilization, 15.29 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in a 72.8% yield. For rGb-C-Dots conjugate, 15.0 mg of rGb were reacted with 6.0 mg of C-Dots; after purification and lyophilization, 17.46 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in an 83.1% yield. For HT-C-Dots conjugate, 15.0 mg of HT were reacted with 6.0 mg of C-Dots; after purification and lyophilization, 17.8 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in an 84.8% yield. For HSA-C-Dots conjugate, 15.0 mg of HSA were reacted with 6.0 mg of C-Dots; after purification and lyophilization, 18.2 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in an 86.7% yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Syntheses and characterization of protein-PEG conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: A typical procedure is as follows (A1AT-PEG conjugates): To a phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (1.0 mL, pH = 7.4) of PEG (10.0 mg) at ambient temperature was added a PBS solution (1.0 mL) of A1AT (5.0 mg). The resulted solution was stirred for 10 min before a PBS solution (0.5 mL) of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 6.0 mg) was added dropwise. The mixture was then allowed to stir at the same temperature for 2 h before it was subjected to a size-exclusion column for separation and purification. The elution from the size-exclusion column was collected into small vials and then characterized by fluorescence spectroscopy (Horiba Jobin Yvon Fluorolog–3) before used in the circular dichroism spectroscopy study (JASCO J–810 spectropolarimeter). After lyophilization, 14.07 mg of A1AT-PEG conjugate were obtained, resulting in a 93.8% yield. The conjugates of PEG with other four proteins were synthesized following the same procedures: for Hb-PEG conjugate, 6.0 mg of Hb were reacted with 10.0 mg of PEG; after purification and lyophilization, 12.75 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in a 79.7% yield. For rGb- PEG conjugate, 6.0 mg of rGb were reacted with 10.0 mg of PEG; after purification and lyophilization, 13.72 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in an 85.8% yield. For HT- PEG conjugate, 6.0 mg of HT were reacted with 10.0 mg of PEG; after purification and lyophilization, 15.6 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in a 97.5% yield. For HSA- PEG conjugate, 6.0 mg of HSA were reacted with 10.0 mg of PEG; after purification and lyophilization, 15.0 mg of conjugate were obtained, resulting in a 93.8% yield.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selection of model drug carriers and protein drugs
ce:section.ce:para: Two drug carriers, namely polymer O-(2-carboxyethyl) polyethylene glycol (PEG) and carbon dots (C-Dots) were chosen as the model carriers for this study. Numerous polymer based DDS have been developed with improved stability and pharmacokinetic properties [21–23]. As a matter of fact, PEG is among the most widely used materials for designing a DDS [24]. As a nontoxic nanomaterial, C-Dots have recently attracted a lot of attention in the biomedical fields due to their great potential in biomedical applications such as drug delivery, bioimaging and theranostics [20,25,26]. Then, five proteins that are closely related to human health were selected as the model substrates, including α1-antitrypsin (A1AT), hemoglobin human (Hb), human serum albumin (HSA), human transferrin (HT) and r-globulin (rGb). A1AT is a 52 kDa serpin and it is generally considered to be among the most prominent candidates to treat α1-antitrypsin deficiency and type 1 diabetes mellitus [27,28]. Hb [29], HSA [30] and HT [31] have been known to support and maintain very important functions for human health. It has been reported that rGb is an effective drug towards the treatment of inflammatory muscle diseases such as polymyositis and dermatomyositis [32,33]. Furthermore, a large class of r-globulins (i.e., immunoglobulins), is widely used for therapy in immunocompromised patients [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD spectra of free proteins and protein-drug carrier conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the connection between the concentrations of a protein drug and its CD signals, aqueous solutions of A1AT with concentrations ranging from 0.0125 to 0.2 mg/ml were studied by CD spectroscopy first. As can be seen from the spectra, the CD signals of A1AT become stronger as the concentrations increase (Fig. 1 A). This finding establishes a positive correlation between the concentrations and CD signals of A1AT, making the determination of A1AT concentration based on its CD signal possible. To ensure the presence of drug carriers PEG and C-Dots do not interfere with the analysis of A1AT based on CD spectroscopy, the CD spectra of A1AT at various concentrations (0.0125–0.2 mg/ml) were measured in the presence of PEG (0.05 mg/ml) and C-Dots (0.05 mg/ml). As expected, the CD signals of A1AT become stronger as its concentrations increase in the presence of both PEG and C-Dots (Fig. 1B and C). A close look at the CD spectra of free A1AT (Fig. 1A), A1AT with PEG (Fig. 1B) and A1AT with C-Dots (Fig. 1C) reveals that these three spectra are almost identical to each other, indicating no obvious interference from the drug carriers PEG and C-Dots when analyzing the protein drug, A1AT, using CD spectroscopy. The concentration of PEG and C-Dots could go as high as 0.1 mg/ml without any interference in the CD spectra of A1AT. Similar results were observed in the study of the other four proteins: Hb, rGb, HSA and HT (see Supplementary Material for details).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calibration curves for protein concentration determination
ce:section.ce:para: Having established the qualitative correlation between the protein concentrations and its CD spectra, the CD signal intensities of the proteins at 208 nm (218 nm for rGb) were then chosen to quantitatively analyze the intensities against the concentrations of proteins. Based on the analysis, calibration curves correlating protein concentrations with CD signals were successfully established (Fig. 2 ). As shown in the graph, the calibration curves for pure A1AT (Fig. 2A, black line), A1AT with PEG (Fig. 2A, red line) and A1AT with C-Dots (Fig. 2A, blue line) overlap well with each other; they are all perfectly linear with a slope ranging from −204.8 to −207.4 (Fig. 2A). Based on the calibration curves, we are confident to conclude that the presence of PEG or C-Dots does not interfere with the CD signals of A1AT, and its analysis using CD spectroscopy. Calibration curves were also established for experiments using the other four proteins (Hb and rGb, see Fig. 2B and C, HSA and HT, see Supplementary Material).

With the successful establishment of the calibration curves, A1AT was covalently loaded to the drug carriers PEG, and C-Dots, respectively. To exclude the interference from the free carriers when considering the encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity for PEG and C-Dots, the as obtained mixtures were loaded to a size exclusion column, separated and purified to yield the A1AT-PEG and A1AT-C-Dots conjugates. The solutions of the conjugates were diluted if necessary so that the concentrations would fall into the range of the calibration curves. After the conjugation, purification and dilution, the solutions of A1AT-PEG (40.0 ml) and A1AT-C-Dots (46.0 ml) conjugates were analyzed by CD spectroscopy to determine the amount of A1AT that had been loaded to PEG and C-Dots (Fig. 3 , black curves).

Based on the CD spectra of A1AT-PEG conjugate (Fig. 3A, black curves) and A1AT-C-Dots conjugate (Fig. 3B, black curves), the concentrations of A1AT in the solutions of A1AT-PEG and A1AT-C-Dots conjugates were determined to be 0.114 mg/ml and 0.100 mg/ml, respectively. According to the concentrations, the amounts of A1AT in its PEG and C-Dots conjugates were calculated to be 4.56 mg and 4.60 mg, respectively (Table 1 ). Since there is no direct way to determine the amount of drug carriers PEG and C-Dots in the corresponding conjugates, they were determined indirectly based on the difference of the total mass of the conjugates and the mass of the proteins. The total mass of A1AT-PEG conjugate was determined to be 14.07 mg while that for A1AT-C-Dots conjugate was 6.05 mg using lyophilization technique. The amounts of PEG and C-Dots in the conjugates, as the difference of the total mass and the mass of proteins, were easily determined to be 9.51 and 1.45 mg (Table 1), respectively. Compositions of conjugates of the other four proteins with PEG and C-Dots were also successfully analyzed following the same method mentioned here (see Supplementary Material for details).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity determination
ce:section.ce:para:
It is widely accepted [13,35] that the encapsulation efficiency (EE%) of a DDS is defined according to the following equation:
(1)
E E % =
W
l o a d
W
t o t a l
∗ 100 %

And the drug-to-carrier ratio is defined as the loading capacity (LC%) according to the following equation:
(2)
L C % =
W
l o a d
W
c a r r i e r
∗ 100 %
where Wtotal is the total amount of drug added to a DDS, Wload is the amount of drug actually loaded to the carrier and Wcarrier is the amount of carrier that carries drug. Using data from Table 1, the encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity of PEG on A1AT were determined to be 91.2 and 47.9%, respectively, according to equations (1) and (2); while that of C-Dots on A1AT were 92.0 and 317.2%, respectively (Table 2 , A1AT). The encapsulation efficiencies and loading capacities of PEG and C-Dots on the other four proteins (Hb, HSA, HT and rGb) were determined similarly and summarized in Table 2.

According to Table 2, the encapsulation efficiencies of PEG and C-Dots on all of the five proteins investigated are very close to each other (maximum difference, 21.3% for Hb). This is no surprise since the conjugation of these proteins with PEG and C-Dots were done via same type of reaction. However, the loading capacities of C-Dots are much (about 5–8 times) higher than that of PEG for the proteins tested (Table 2, loading capacity). According to our acid-base titration experiment, it was estimated that there are about 5.8 mmol of carboxylic group available in each gram of C-Dots. While only 0.29 mmol of carboxylic group is available with each gram of PEG based on its structure analysis. Thus, the distinctive loading capacity difference between C-Dots and PEG could be attributed to the fact that C-Dots have much more carboxylic groups in per unit mass than PEG does. Based on the study, one can expect C-Dots have a great potential as a drug carrier in the targeted drug delivery. With its significant loading capacity (i.e., high drug-to-carrier ratio), only a small amount of C-Dots would be required to deliver a specific amount of drug to site of interest, potentially minimizing the carrier-related toxicity and immune reactions.

In the study, five different proteins that are closely related to human health (α1-antitrypsin, hemoglobin human, human serum albumin, human transferring and r-globulin) were covalently loaded to two promising drug carriers, polyethylene glycol (molecular weight – 3000) and carbon dots (∼4 nm). The presence of these drug carriers did not interfere with the CD spectra of the proteins as long as the concentration of the carriers is not too high to allow accurate measurement of the CD signals. We were able to determine the compositions of the protein-carrier conjugates based on calibration curves from CD spectra, which are not possible using UV–vis absorbance or dye-binding assays based traditional analytical methods due to the interference of drug carriers. With this new method, the encapsulation efficiencies and loading capacities of PEG and C-Dots on the five different proteins were successfully determined. The method provides a simple yet convenient solution to the current challenges in the quantification of drug delivery systems. As the needs for innovative drug delivery systems increase, the analytical method developed here will find broader applications, especially in the areas where traditional methods do not work efficiently.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from King Abdulaziz University, Kingdom of Saudi Arabia, and University of Miami, USA.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2016.08.014.
